key_insights.insight: Confidence can be trained by mastering one small thing first and building from there.
key_insights.quote: The easiest way to learn confidence is to be confident about one thing...Show me that that's better than anybody else's. And the minute you can be confident about one thing, now you can be confident about two things.

key_insights.insight: Observational skills are crucial for effective management and understanding people.
key_insights.quote: How good are you at observing? ... People, events, opportunities.

key_insights.insight: A candidate's demeanor in an interview can signal their level of confidence and options.
key_insights.quote: Their ability to be so relaxed in that environment and kind of own the chair in my boardroom was actually, it actually made me kind of wants them more because they were signaling to me that they had lots of options.

key_insights.insight: Confidence is conveyed through a deeper voice and avoiding rising inflections at the end of sentences.
key_insights.quote: A confident person doesn't have to talk fast and doesn't talk high. No is always said down. No.

key_insights.insight: Speaking in cadence allows listeners to process information and attach emotion to the message.
key_insights.quote: When you speak in cadence...people listen, they have time to process what you're saying, but they can also attach the emotion that goes with it.

key_insights.insight: Hand gestures, specifically finger positioning, reveal underlying emotions like care or fear.
key_insights.quote: The more confident we are, the further our fingers are. I care...When we fear, our fingers come together and when we fear a lot, our thumbs tuck in.

key_insights.insight: When interviewing someone, focus on the problems they have solved, not just their technical skills.
key_insights.quote: Give me a list of the problems you have solved. Most people, when they hire, they never ask that question.

actionable_advice.advice: Practice saying 'no' in a lower, more authoritative tone.
actionable_advice.how_to: Record yourself saying 'no' repeatedly, focusing on lowering your vocal pitch and avoiding a rising inflection.

actionable_advice.advice: Become an expert in a specific area to boost your confidence.
actionable_advice.how_to: Identify a topic you're interested in and dedicate time to studying it thoroughly.  Share your knowledge with others to solidify your understanding.

actionable_advice.advice: Observe people's body language to understand their emotions and intentions.
actionable_advice.how_to: Watch videos of public speakers or negotiators and analyze their nonverbal cues. Practice identifying emotions based on facial expressions, posture, and gestures.

actionable_advice.advice: Occupy your entitled space to project confidence.
actionable_advice.how_to: Be aware of your posture and gestures in meetings. Avoid shrinking or hunching over, and use open, expressive hand movements.

memorable_stories: Joe Navarro's experience as a young FBI agent struggling with his voice and learning to project authority.
The anecdote about teaching a Harvard class to say 'no' with authority and a lower vocal tone.
Recounting his experience as a refugee from Cuba arriving in the US.
contrarian_claims: The idea that confidence is innate is challenged; it's argued that confidence is a skill that can be trained.
episode_title: The Diary Of A CEO with Steven Bartlett - Most Replayed Moment: Confidence Can Be Taught! Use These Body Language Cues To Your Advantage!
